[[RunningHead]]

The Evolution of the Emotion-Processing Mind. By Robert Langs. Karnac. £18.95. Pp. xvi + 223 and Death Anxiety and Clinical Practice. Also by Robert Langs. Karnac. £19.95. Pp. xiii + 250.

Phil Mollon

Phil Mollon

Phil Mollon  

One of the endorsements on the jacket cover of Death Anxiety aptly describes Robert Langs as ‘an esteemed maverick’. I have long been an admirer of Langs's uncompromising pursuit of his disconcerting clinical observations and ensuing theories—and still remember the sense of shock, disorientation and illumination on reading his book The Listening Process over twenty years ago. Langs's message can be overwhelming in its implications for understanding human communication, both in the consulting-room and in life in general—and that is the point. Human beings have, according to Langs, evolved a conscious mind which is protected from the frightening perceptions available to the unconscious emotion-processing mind, precisely because these are overwhelming.

Langs believes that the unconscious emotion-processing mind is developmentally and phylogenetically primary. This system accurately perceives the raw and primitive emotional intentions of others as well as those of the self. In order to deal with the emotional overload of this primary system, there evolved the conscious system which maintains substantial defences against disturbing emotional perceptions. A common observation is that borderline and psychotic patients, whose defences are less stable, are more inclined sometimes to perceive consciously the unconscious harmful intentions of others. Langs's view of the clinical process reverses the more traditional emphasis upon the patient's transferential displacements from other (childhood) figures onto the therapist; instead he draws attention to the ubiquitous tendency for patients to displace onto figures outside therapy their frightening perceptions of, and reactions to, the therapist's remarks and behaviours. The unconscious emotion-processing system appears to be exquisitely attuned to boundary and frame deviations—Langs points out that the maintenance of boundaries is fundamental to life at all levels. Such deviations may be consciously welcomed, whilst unconsciously perceived as highly damaging.

[[RunningHead]]

Reflections on Purchasing Psychotherapy Services: The Importance of Unconscious Factors

Antony Garelick

Antony Garelick

London, E17 8HP.

Antony Garelick  

The ambivalence towards psychotherapy services, underlying oedipal anxieties and the sense of exclusion from the therapeutic dyad, is explored. Their significance and the role of unconscious factors and their interplay with organisational dynamics are discussed, drawing on personal experience. Their importance in the purchasing of psychotherapy services is considered and the development of helpful strategies is described.

responseSet.title: Editorial
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Editorial

Jane Milton

Jane Milton

Jane Milton  

Our papers in this issue of Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy span the life-cycle, with reports of work with a child and his mother, with staff caring for adult populations of psychotic and borderline patients, and with a team caring for the elderly mentally ill. O'Connor is in the all-too-unusual position of a psychoanalyst who maintains her identity as a general psychiatrist, heading a team caring for psychotic patients on the intensive-care unit of a general psychiatric hospital. Her psychoanalytic perspective, rather than being in opposition to a drug-based model, helps staff to use medication more appropriately and effectively instead of reactively, as her clinical examples show.

Much as O'Connor is concerned with introducing dimensions of meaning into an over-rigid medical model, Evans is concerned with a debased and formulaic use of certain psychotherapeutic ideas. Again, on the wards he describes, staff for reasons of comfort and familiarity have stopped listening and thinking about what is going on between them and their patients, and can benefit from a fresh outside view.

Giving lively and detailed clinical material from parallel work with a child and his mother, Barth argues for the value of his model. In this work he has to bear in mind the complex meshing of intrapsychic and interpersonal dynamics, his binocular view both enriching and multiplying the complexity of his work.

At the other end of life, Ardern and colleagues describe some work in Old Age Psychiatry teams where a psychoanalytic perspective is available. This report is welcome and timely as the Old Age Psychiatry section of the APP develops.

Finally, Sigrell's team from Sweden uses Malan's model for researching dynamic change to assess outcome in ten patients. The incorporation of this rigorous and clinically-meaningful outcome project into a psychotherapy training is to be applauded.

1

The Baby and the Bathwater. Karnac. £17.95. International Universities Press. $35.00. Pp. xviii + 172.

Nicola Blandford

Nicola Blandford

Nicola Blandford  

Dr Nina Coltart died on June 24 1997. This, her final book, is a collection of essays, many of which have been published before, but including her first and last papers. It is a summary of her professional development and an account of her approach to the work of a British Psychoanalyst in the latter part of the twentieth century.

Coltart qualified as a doctor at St Bartholomew's, London, after first reading Modern Languages at Oxford. In 1969 she became a full member of the British Psycho-Analytical Society and went on to give service as training analyst, supervisor, teacher and administrator, as well as ten years as Director of the London Clinic of Psycho-Analysis. Rather bravely Coltart acknowledged that she preferred the practice of psychotherapy to that of psychoanalysis. She specialised in consultations for diagnosis and assessment, and over a period of thirty years did over 3,000. This obviously required a good network of therapists to refer patients on to, and a responsibility to be aware of the strengths and weaknesses of those therapists.

Of the ten essays here, all but one were recently written. The exception is the first, ‘The Man with Two Mothers’, an account of her treatment of a very damaged man with a perversion. This was her qualifying paper to the Society; she can comment that perhaps it now seems old-fashioned in its classically Freudian language, but she makes the important point that it can be very helpful to have to treat a perverse patient early in one's career. It is a clear and moving clinical account.

79

responseSet.title: Editorial
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Editorial

Jane Milton

Jane Milton

Jane Milton  

For this issue of Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy we focus on two important conferences. Firstly, we publish two papers submitted (and peer-reviewed in the usual way) by Garelick and Taylor. These are from talks given at two linked conference days in March 1997 devoted to the implications of the then newly-published Department of Health Review of Psychotherapy Services. We asked Nicholas Temple to provide some context by outlining the main content and some of the implications of this Review, which we hope will be helpful for those less familiar with this document and its potentially far-reaching consequences, both positive and negative. Taylor's paper focuses thoughtfully on the language and tone of the Review, which, he argues, reveals some unhelpfully moralistic, superego aspects of it, which somewhat distorts its more constructive aspects. Garelick, rather than addressing the content of the Review directly, addresses some of the pressures, conscious and unconscious, that might prompt such repeated, radical investigations of, and sometimes attacks on, our work in psychotherapy departments. He is intrigued by the huge furore often generated by what he elegantly shows is such a relatively tiny and inexpensive branch of the Health Service.

In the second section of this issue, we took the decision to put aside our usual format, in order to publish the proceedings of the Mind's Eye conference, held at Moorfields Hospital last October. The background to this conference is as follows. Funded by the Inman Trust of the Institute of Psycho-Analysis, and held at the Tavistock Clinic, a short-term pilot seminar for professionals working with eye disorders was led by Alexis Brook, psychoanalytic psychotherapist, Andrew Elder, consultant in general practice and primary care, and Sotiris Zalidis, general practitioner and, like Andrew Elder, a member of the Balint Society. Seminar members included Richard Wormald, consultant ophthalmologist, Liz Middleton, orthoptist, and Mandy O'Keefe, social worker; and the enthusiasm generated was such that after the seminar had ended they were joined by Jennifer Johns, psychoanalyst and Trustee of the Inman Trust, to plan a conference where perspectives on the myriad psychological aspects of eye disorder could be shared by a multidisciplinary professional group. As a participant myself, I can vouch for the optimistic and collaborative atmosphere generated. The audience included many representatives of all the professions represented on the planning committee, and in addition optometrists, ophthalmic nurses, workers with the blind, psychotherapists, counsellors and others. A number of clinical and research initiatives have already been suggested arising from the day. There was a strong wish that the proceedings be published, our journal being one obvious possibility for this. We felt that this was an important example of the potential for the application of psychoanalytic thinking in a Health Service setting which has great scope for development.

responseSet.title: One Eye Sees, the other Feels
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

One Eye Sees, the other Feels

Simon Horgan

Simon Horgan

Simon Horgan   Specialist Registrar, Moorfields Eye Hospital

Introduction

The title of this talk is taken from the writings of Paul Klee (1879-1940), the Swiss painter and diarist who eventually died of scleroderma. That ‘one eye sees, the other feels’ sums up the elaborate integration that exists between the external world and the mind. Perhaps Klee knew, or just guessed correctly, that the majority of the sensory input to the brain originates from the eyes, and, as Nietzsche stated, ‘all evidence of truth comes only from the senses’.

We have heard already how both eyes and their vision offer conduits for anxiety in the sentient being. The subject of this paper is ocular hypochondriasis. It is not one that has been well covered by ophthalmic writers. Somatic conditions—illnesses without physical basis—are easily recognised in patients at this and every other hospital. Little is made of their symptomatology, and almost nothing of their need.

For the benefit of the reader, a clinical example is useful.

Mrs Y was first seen at Moorfields Hospital in 1964, complaining of eyelid irritation. Over the next few years she subsequently presented with other complaints, but on each occasion was found to have healthy eyes. Her persistence was rewarded by examination and appraisal by many different specialists—neurologist, ophthalmologist, psychiatrist—but no foundation for her symptoms had ever been discovered. She continued to attend the Casualty department in this hospital, and has now been seen more than 350 separate times, occasionally twice in a day, and often on consecutive days.

Her GP writes:

… her problem is indeed a chronic anxiety-state with somatic anxiety and has presented through several guises not only related to her eyes but also to leukaemia, multiple sclerosis and Parkinson's disease as well as her apparent dental problem … she has been treated for chronic anxiety for over thirty years now and has been on Librium presumably since it was first introduced.

He goes on to discuss Mrs Y's further management, but concludes that her condition is completely refractory to treatment and is now securely entrenched.

A Practical Guide to Forensic Psychotherapy. Edited by E V Welldon & C Van VelsenJessica Kingsley Publishers. £18.95. Pp. viii + 350.

James Mackeith

James Mackeith

James Mackeith  

This book is the third volume in a series entitled ‘Forensic Focus’. The declared aim is to present theoretical and clinical issues in the field of forensic psychotherapy. The editor of the series was Dr Murray Cox, a distinguished clinical practitioner and author who died shortly after this volume appeared. Dr Cox, together with Dr Christopher Cordess, was the editor of the first volume in the series, Forensic Psychotherapy: Crime, Psychodynamics and the Offender Patient. That substantial volume was the first of its kind.

The Foreword to this book makes clear that it takes the form of essays on the ‘intricacies and complexities encountered in working with the forensic patient’. Apart from Murray Cox's brief introductory Foreword, all the other contributors to this volume, nearly forty of them, happen to be women. Is this an accident?

An introduction makes a number of points, emphasising that forensic psychiatry (psychiatry in relation to the law) and its offspring, forensic psychotherapy, is not ‘soft’ on offenders.

‘The aim is to help locate responsibility by means of understanding within a person rather than in the environment.’

‘Forensic patients uniquely demonstrate their internal worlds as with their crime they act out something of their internal object relations.’

The other parent discipline of forensic psychotherapy is psychoanalytical psychotherapy. In the first part of the book a chapter from Estela Welldon provides a brief overview of a practical approach; and in a second chapter on management and treatment, Cecelia Taylor deals with the wider aspect of how forensic psychotherapy plays a part in the overall strategy of the management and treatment of offender patients.

In Part 2, the main body of this book, short essays by many authors address case-studies in an illuminating way.

283

Hystories: Hysterical Epidemics in Modern Culture. By Elaine Showalter London: Picador. Pp. 244. and Multiple Selves, Multiple Voices: Working with Trauma, Violation and Dissociation. By Phil Mollon. Chichester: Wiley. Pp. 211.

Cleo Velsen

Cleo Velsen

Cleo Velsen  

A year ago, I was shocked to hear Melvyn Bragg, on his radio programme, Start the Week, shouting ‘hysterically’ at Elaine Showalter for daring to suggest that there is evidence for a psychological explanation for ME, or ‘chronic fatigue syndrome’. People still become furious at the idea that something is ‘only in the mind’, as if mental disorder and psychological distress are invalid. There is a search, and wish, for physical evidence which can, as Showalter puts it, ‘firmly place cause and cure outside the self.’

Showalter's overall argument concerns a ‘premature’ announcement of the death of hysteria, instead suggesting that its expression is what is different:

In the 1990s, the United States has become the hot zone of psychogenic diseases, new and mutating forms of hysteria amplified by modem communications and fin-de-siècle anxiety. Contemporary hysterical patients blame external sources—virus, sexual molestation, chemical warfare, satanic conspiracy, alien infiltration—for psychic problems.

Her book examines her hypothesis that hysteria survives in the 1990s as social stories of our time which are lived out, and, through modern information technology, are much more ‘contagious’ than in the past. This is linked with the ‘more generalised paranoias, religious revivals, and conspiracy theories’ plus the ‘apocalyptic anxieties that always accompany the end of the century.’ Showalter describes a pattern where the message of an initial well-meaning and authoritative crusader of a condition—for example, a doctor—is picked up by the media from which people then learn of the new ‘disease’. This is followed by unconscious development of symptoms and the attraction of more media attention, in a cycle.

For many, the term ‘hysteria’ is pejorative, associated as it is with being over-emotional, irresponsible and feminine; feminists argue that it has been used to trivialise women's medical and political complaints. As a feminist herself, Showalter believes that hysteria is ‘inevitably a feminist issue, because for centuries doctors regarded it as a female reproductive disease.’ She points out how women outnumber men in almost all categories of unexplained illness apart from Gulf War syndrome: for example 80-90% of those with chronic fatigue syndrome, those who claim recovered memories of abuse, those recounting satanic ritual abuse, and those diagnosed with MPD, are women. Showalter's volume begins with a useful, and well written, history of hysteria, and she importantly provides a socio-historical context as well as a feminist dimension.

Turning a Blind Eye: Some Reflections on a Multidisciplinary Seminar

Andrew Elder

Andrew Elder

Andrew Elder   GP & Consultant in General Practice & Primary Care, Tavistock Clinic

I am going to introduce the work of the seminar by briefly describing its origins; and in doing so I hope to indicate some of the perspectives through which the work might be viewed. Two colleagues from the seminar, an orthoptist and a social worker, will then describe cases that were discussed, so that the work of the seminar can speak for itself through the reflections of the two of the participants.

The multi-disciplinary seminar to which the title refers was held at the Tavistock Clinic between February and July 1996, and included the following thirteen participants: a consultant psychotherapist, two social workers, three orthoptists, a consultant paediatrician, an optometrist, a consultant ophthalmologist, a research registrar in ophthalmology, and three general practitioners.

Twenty meetings were held, each lasting one and a half hours, at weekly intervals. The work was based on the presentation of current cases. Forty-four patients were discussed, some on several occasions; and they represented a wide range of severity and variety of problems, with an approximately equal distribution of cases in which there was a physical basis to the disorder, and those in which no clear physical basis had been identified.

The seminars were planned as a pilot project, an exploratory venture, and not as teaching events. They were to be based on mutual learning: between the individuals participating, and between members of different disciplines who seldom discuss cases together in any detail. The focus was on our shared understanding of individual cases, and on the kind of psychological help that can be given within the various ophthalmic settings in which the participants worked.

How did it Start?

This morning we have heard something about the rather few clinicians who have had an interest in the psychosomatic aspects of eye disease and ocular symptoms, starting either from an ophthalmological or from a psychoanalytic perspective; and who of course included Inman, who uniquely combined both. The stimulus for our work came from Alexis Brook's interest in the subject, and his experiences in seeing eye-patients referred to him by doctors, first in an Eye department in Portsmouth, and later with Sotiris Zalidis and his partners in their general practice in Hackney.

Specific Techniques for the Psychotherapy of Schizophrenic Patients. By Andrew Lotterman. Madison, Connecticut: International Universities Press. $37.50. Pp. xii + Pp. 224.

Brian Martindale

Brian Martindale

Brian Martindale  

This is a most important and encouraging book for psychoanalytic psychotherapists working in the Health Service who wish to contribute directly to the treatment of some of those patients suffering from schizophrenic illnesses.

Its importance stems from the following factors:

unlike a number of accounts of psychoanalysts working with psychotic patients, there is no room for doubt that the patients discussed in this book are suffering from long-standing schizophrenic illnesses that have been fully recognised as such by general psychiatrists;

Lotterman is clearly deeply respectful of non-analytic contributions to the management and treatment of schizophrenic disturbances; and

most importantly (and perhaps for the first time in a book written by a psychoanalyst) Lotterman offers psychoanalytic psychotherapists a number of specific recommendations and techniques for particular manifestations of schizophrenic disturbance—such as delusions, hallucinations, boundary disturbances, pressure of speech and looseness of associations, and other disturbances of thought. This contrasts with a number of other publications in which the technique seems not to differ from psychoanalytic work with other types of disturbance.

Though some of these techniques will be controversial, they will surely provoke both intense interest and close observation of the clinical consequences and also debate amongst psychotherapists as the pendulum begins to swing back to a better integration of the biological and psychological approaches.

Lotterman clearly has extensive clinical experience of working with schizophrenic patients. I thought that he was particularly good in describing the patient's disturbances with conceptual and symbolic thinking, and the unconscious motivations that may drive this. I found his practical recommendations and his theoretical exposition convincing and fully cognizant of the various contributors in this field. He emphasises that it must be the primary task in the early phases of treatment (and at times of psychotic regression) to try to find ways of assisting the patient to channel ‘hard-to-bear’ affects and ideas back into communicable thoughts. It is this interference with conceptual and symbolic thinking which is the reason why classical interpretative work alone may not be possible in the early phases of treatment as that method depends so greatly on these functions being intact.

[[RunningHead]]

Notes on the Psychotherapy Review

Nicholas Temple

Nicholas Temple

120 Belsize Lane, London, NW3 5BA.

Nicholas Temple  

In September 1996, the NHS Executive published a document reviewing psychotherapy services in England (Parry & Richardson 1996). This followed an extensive review process which was originally set up to examine psychotherapy provision, and the role of the Tavistock Clinic. However, it has ended up as strongly endorsing the important role of psychotherapy and psychological therapies in the treatment of mental health problems and has adopted a broad approach to psychotherapy. It is the first time that a national report has been prepared which endorses psychotherapeutic work in such a comprehensive way.

The Review sets a framework of three types of psychotherapy delivery:

Type A: Psychological treatment as an integral component of mental health care

Type B: Eclectic Psychological therapy and counselling

Type C: Formal Psychotherapy

These types of psychotherapy do not describe levels of skill alone, but relate to the patient's needs and the setting. For example, Type A treatment will be a component of all mental health work, and, as such, every mental health professional should be able to offer this. At present, this is often not the case. Type B might involve a short course of structured therapy of a limited range, and this is commonly practised in the NHS. The quality of supervision and training will have a major effect on the outcome of this sort of work. Type C is specialist psychotherapy, such as practised by child psychotherapists, adult medical psychotherapists, etc.

The Review's survey found good evidence for the effectiveness of a range of psychotherapies which can help many mental health problems, including severe mental illness.

The research report (Fonagy & Roth 1996), published separately, endorses this, but points out the need for more research in the effectiveness of psychoanalytic psychotherapy.

The Roth & Fonagy review of outcome research has the drawback that it examines only a very limited range of research studies. These have to be randomised controlled trials with homogeneous groups of treated patients. Thus, studies representing the typical population for which psychoanalytic psychotherapy is offered, a mixed population suffering from a variety of neurotic difficulties and personality disorders, are not represented at all in the literature survey. The requirement for strict control-group format, rather than the less statistically powerful comparison group or longitudinal own-control research design, also inevitably excludes much of the psychoanalytic psychotherapy research literature (see Milton 1996).

Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy and Outcome Research: A Qualitative Study

Bo Sigrell , Ann Cornell , Karin Gyllensköld , Ingrid Lindgren and Per Stenfelt

Per Stenfelt

Björngårdsgatan 25118 52 Stockholm.

Per Stenfelt  

As part of a Swedish postgraduate training programme in psychotherapy, a qualitative approach based on psychodynamic criteria was used to evaluate the results of psychoanalytically-oriented individual psychotherapy. The study extended over six years and involved ten patients who had undergone two years of individual therapy. Based primarily on patient interviews prior to therapy and follow-up interviews some two to four years after the therapy had ended, the success of the therapy was rated by trainees in the programme and by the patients themselves. The Malan/Tavistock outcome scale for dynamic change was followed.

Some Thoughts on the Relationship between Eye Pain and Emotion

Sotiris Zalidis

Sotiris Zalidis

Sotiris Zalidis   GP and Member of the Balint Society

‘If thy right eye offend thee pluck it out’ (Matt. Chap. V, v.29).

Introduction

Patients who present to the general practitioner with eye pain are frightened that they may have a serious disease which might lead to loss of the eye, or vision or both. Yet most cases of eye pain remain medically unexplained and may be referred to as functional syndromes or somatoform disorders. This does not mean, however, that it is all in the mind. The so-called functional symptoms occur in the context of emotional arousal, and the contemporary way of understanding emotions recognises that they are both biological and psychological at the same time. In this theoretical framework most functional somatic symptoms can be explained by the physiological mechanisms that mediate emotional arousal.

[[RunningHead]]

An Analytic Approach in a Psychiatric Intensive Care Unit

Siobhan O'Connor

Siobhan O'Connor

16 Newtownbreda Road, Belfast, BT8 4AS.

Siobhan O'Connor  

This paper gives a psychoanalytic perspective on work in an Intensive Care ward of a general psychiatric hospital. It is a twenty-bedded locked ward, which had previously had limited medical input. Rigid adherence to a medical model had led to inappropriate reliance on the use of drugs, which can be shown to have perpetuated many of the problems, and in particular violent behaviour. A psychoanalytic hypothesis, using the concept of idealisation, helps to make sense of the denial of the limitations of working within a medical model. My hypothesis is that violent behaviour in a ward setting gives rise to confusion of boundaries and defensive reactions against primitive fears. Fears of chaos and escalating destructiveness in behaviour can stimulate a wish for an ideal solution to cope with the violence. With idealisation, there is denial of the boundaries of an external reality: in this case, the limitations of pharmacotherapy. This encompasses denial of the toxic side-effects of medication. Chronic illness can be difficult to tolerate, and the frustration of the professional can lead to a displacement aggression towards the patient, enacted by toxic prescription of drugs. Clinical examples illustrate the resistances in staff to making changes in therapeutic approach. By illustrating this work, I hope to demonstrate the advantage of an analytic approach in psychiatry.

[[RunningHead]]

What Do Psychoanalysts Want? The Problem of Aims in Psychoanalytic Therapy. By Joseph Sandler and Anna Ursula Dreher. Routledge £37.50 (hb), £13.99 (pb). Pp. 141.

Rosemary Davies

Rosemary Davies

Rosemary Davies  

In this short and scholarly book, Sandler & Dreher take up one of the vexed issues of psychoanalysis and offer us what Arnold Cooper describes in his preface as ‘a radical critique of our own self-satisfaction’. They make it clear from the outset that they do not expect to solve the puzzle through a unifying theory of aims, but rather they offer a theory about aims which they hope will offer clarification.

They choose a historical approach which helps the reader find their bearings and which, as the authors argue, seems the only viable approach. They present their review of the development of thinking on aims of psychoanalysis decade by decade, after an initial chapter on Freud's own views. Thus they trace: the early Freudians in the 1920s; consolidation in the pre-war decade; the emigration of analysts and a period of transition; the 1950s and the widening-scope discussions; heightening tensions; the 1970s and the flowering of pluralism; and pragmatism and integration in contemporary psychoanalysis. The chapter on Freud's view of aims reminds us again how he kept in mind so many issues which have puzzled us since, and furthermore how he could countenance and acknowledge his own changing views. Sandler & Dreher's general historical account depicts a move from analytic hubris to a more limited view of what can be achieved. Freud did this in his own lifetime, shifting from what the authors describe as

an optimistic, at times even idealistic, view of what analysis can achieve [to] … a more realistic (one could say pessimistic) attitude in ‘Analysis Terminable and Interminable’

(p. 22)

Kate Barrows

Kate Barrows

Kate Barrows  

Since weekly infant observation was initiated by Esther Bick some forty years ago as a cornerstone of the pre-clinical training for psychoanalytic candidates at the Institute of Psycho-Analysis and child psychotherapy trainees at the Tavistock Clinic, many child and adult psychoanalytic and psychotherapy organisations worldwide have included it in their training. It had therefore had an enormous influence on psychoanalytical practice, showing to observers not only the subtleties and intensities of the early relationship between the infant and his or her mother, father and family, but also the value of containment without interpretation, of observation without intervention. It is striking that infant observation has not, until recent years, had much of a public voice but for a long time has, like the observer, remained relatively silent. Infant Observation is part of a movement which seeks to articulate and publish some of the findings in this fertile area. Several books on the subject have also been published in the last ten years (see bibliography on page 283).

This new journal will be published three times a year, and each edition will have two different Guest Editors; for this edition they are Susan Reid and Michael Rustin, who have also contributed papers. The journal fills an important gap, since there is none other devoted specifically to this topic. The aim seems to be to place infant observation fairly and squarely on the map as more than a preparatory requirement for psychotherapy and psychoanalytic trainings. Infant observation is seen as making significant contributions to theory and practice in its own right.

Michael Rustin in his contribution stakes a claim for infant observation as a research tool, based mainly upon detailed observational accounts, and likens it to the kind of research undertaken by psychoanalysts who use the consulting-room as a ‘laboratory’, and their case-descriptions as the basis for theoretical advances. He points out that a major difference in observational studies is the lack of a therapeutic dialogue between observer and observed, and the fact of there being no direct response to the observational process; so there may be less confirmation or discomfirmation of explanatory hypotheses. However, he cites Bick's seminal paper on second-skin phenomena which utilised infant observation in making a significant contribution to psychoanalytic theory and to clinical practice, notably to thinking about primitive defences. He also points out that infant observation can further the study of particular types of infants (such as Piontelli's studies of twins), and that it can develop a study of the variances between thriving and non-thriving infants of various kinds.

[[RunningHead]]

Why the Cycle in a Cyclical Psychosis? An Analytic Contribution to the Understanding of Recurrent Manic-Depressive Psychosis

Richard Lucas

Richard Lucas

St Ann's Road, London N15 3TH

Richard Lucas  

In this paper, the psychopathology underlying recurrent manic-depressive psychosis is considered. In the introduction, it is noted that the opportunities for prolonged analytic case-studies of severe psychotic disorders have become more difficult since the closure of the specialist analytically oriented in-patient units in the NHS and the changes at Chestnut Lodge in the United States.

Material is presented from an analytic case-study of a patient with recurrent manic-depressive psychosis, followed in the National Health Service over a fifteen-year period. The material allows the reader to follow the recurrent nature of the psychopathology. Consideration is given as to why the analysis led to an intensification of the manic episodes, and the role played by defences against experiencing guilt in maintaining manic states of mind. Some recent improvement is related to the emergence of a third position, a shared reflectiveness between analyst and patient in helping the patient to live with and manage the manic episodes.

Whether or not a satisfactory therapeutic outcome is produced, it is argued that psychoanalytic studies of severe psychotic disorders not only deepen one's understanding, but also help in everyday general psychiatry with seemingly intractable states of mind.

Both past and more recent contributions from the psychoanalytic literature are reviewed for the light they shed on the clinical material and the condition in general.

Self-Injury to the Eyes—A Discussion

Jane Milton

Jane Milton

Jane Milton   Psychoanalyst & Consultant Psychotherapist, Tavistock Clinic

My first introduction to this shocking and apparently incomprehensible form of self-mutilation was as a junior psychiatrist, when I was asked to an Eye ward to see a psychotic who had enucleated one of his eyes, damaged the other, and tried (unsuccessfully) to cut off his penis. More recently, I supervised the NHS psychotherapeutic treatment of a disturbed woman who, it emerged, had a secret compulsion to her face and eyes, causing corneal scarring which had been puzzling her GP and ophthalmologist. This latter case has been beautifully described by the psychotherapist, Margaret G, who is here today, and her paper, published in the British Journal of Psychotherapy two years ago, has contributed helpfully to my thinking about this subject.

The thirty or so relevant articles from the psychoanalytic, psychiatric and ophthalmological literature I have unearthed, give a fascinating example of the way our professions interrelate. This is something of which I know Patrick Trevor-Roper has long been aware; and I found helpful and thought-provoking a review article of his written in 1980, ‘The Psychopathic Eye’, in which he describes the eye as ‘a sounding-board for the travails of the whole persona’. I am going to talk about patients who attack their eyes, either grossly and openly, or in a more hidden, repetitive ways. A psychoanalytic perspective may help to make sense of such acts, dealing as it does with the inner world of primitive structures and relationships. I hope to lead us in to a discussion of how we can work together both to understand and to help such patients.

I will start by telling you about some typical patients described in the literature. Gross attempts at self-blinding, usually by stabbing one or both eyes, are uncommon, but regularly described. A 1994 review by Kennedy & Feldmann identified forty-five known cases in the literature up until then. Most patients described have been young adult male schizophrenics. Their fears and delusions, when enquired into, seem to concern themes of perverse or forbidden sex, often with incestuous overtones, coupled with guilty terror that they have offended God. The patient I saw, for example, had, either in delusion or in reality, I never knew, been sleeping with his mother, and was convinced that he had to atone violently for this crime. The literature on more subtle forms of eye damage is more sparse, but I wonder if this is because it is more hidden rather than less common.

About Parallel Work with Latency Children and Their Parents

Daniel Barth

Daniel Barth

Schafmatrweg 55a, CH-4102 Binningen, Switzerland.

Daniel Barth  

This paper deals with the role of projective identification in the transmission of psychopathology from generation to generation, and its implications for the treatment of latency children. A case-history of an 8-year-old boy with emotional and cognitive problems is presented. He participated in weekly psychoanalytically-oriented psychotherapy sessions parallel to monthly sessions with his mother. Working separately with mother and son has helped us to understand better the intense projections of each into the other. Interpretation led to symptom-improvement in the boy and to a positive change in their relationship. This kind of intervention differs from the classical individual or family-therapy approaches, and may have implications for the concept of parental guidance in child psychotherapy. This type of work requires a theoretical oscillation between an intrapsychic understanding in the individual setting, and an interpersonal context in work with parents.

On Ageing, Dying, Death and Eternal Life11

Brian Martindale

Brian Martindale

63 Lancaster Road, London W11 1QG.

Brian Martindale  

In this article, some of the developmental issues that are confronted in the later phases of life are discussed. I describe the inevitable (a) reawakening of the internalisation of earlier dependent state, and (b) identifications with the dying experience of key other persons. I show how both points may cloud external reality and give rise to symptoms.

I also dwell on how the difficulty, if not impossibility, for the unconscious to know about nothingness (‘not experiencing’) means that the concept of death cannot be fully grasped. The study of any individual's anticipated ‘life after death’ is therefore potentially of great clinical significance and of relevance to the provision of care for the elderly.

Gill Hinshelwood  

This excellent book is the well-matured fruit of the early pioneering endeavours of Michael Balint, and later and more intensively, of Tom Main. It seems very fitting that it has been published at much the same time as Michael Balint—Object Relations Pure and Applied by Harold Stewart (1996, Routledge). The reader interested in the development of applied psychoanalysis would gain much from reading the books together.

The first page of Blocks and Freedoms in Sexual Life sets out the concern of the author, which is with the search for some truths between the body and the mind. The search is a joint undertaking of doctor and patient, in clinical situations where the patient is distressed by a sexual problem, and may expect a physical aetiology, which is not present.

The book is in three parts, and Part One, Aspects of Doctoring, describes the skills that doctors practising psychosexual medicine need to acquire.

Denise Roberts

Denise Roberts

Denise Roberts  

Fat is a Feminist Issue was published twenty years ago—a refreshingly passionate book that challenged the status quo view of women. In it Orbach described the social and cultural expectations for girls and women of having little direct power, of learning to deny their own needs, but instead facilitating the needs of others. Eating disorders were seen as an area where women might express power and control over their own bodies. The book met with great success, and it seemed to strike a chord for many women. Orbach and colleagues set up the Women's Therapy Centre, which was accessible and ‘user-friendly’. The barrier between status of ‘patient’ and student’ on a self-awareness course was blurred. It has been successful in bringing into treatment deprived and isolated women who might never of gone to traditional organisations, and who may not have been seen as able to use psychoanalytic psychotherapy.

The first of these books is a compilation of introductory seminars on women's psychology run by the Women's Therapy Centre. It seems that a range of views has developed. Orbach summarises very much the same views as twenty years ago, with a particular focus, the vicissitudes of over-closeness and lack of differentiation between mother and daughter. The technique that stems from this is, broadly, a supportive psychotherapy where patients are given much encouragement and support to recognise their own needs and wishes, and take a more active stance in relation to them. Ernst and Maguire take a more recognisably psychodynamic stance, arguing that the first approach goes only so far. They point out that the risk is of endorsing the woman as victim, as locating anything ‘bad’—aggression, envy, desire, sexuality—‘out there’, and that a woman therapist as well as a woman patient may particularly wish to be seen as ‘good’ and have difficulty recognising the negative transference.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Group and the Oedipal Situation

Michael Halton

Michael Halton

9 Dunstan Road, London, NW11 8AG.

Michael Halton  

In this paper, the oedipal situation as outlined by Klein is taken as the starting-point for exploring how psychoanalytic treatment objectives can be pursued through the medium of a group. The two foremost group theorists, Bion and Foulkes, are critically reviewed, and some modifications to their technique are suggested in the light of more recent developments in psychoanalytic theory and technique, particularly Bion's later work on psychosis and thinking.

It is proposed that the central conflicts in group therapy are around desires and fears of inclusion and exclusion centring on the person of the therapist, standing for the desired primary object. It is argued that the paramount concern for group-members functioning at a neurotic level are genital and pregenital oedipal conflicts, where the therapist and the group, or some other pairing, intrapsychically represent the parental couple and the primal scene in all its ramifications.

Contemporary ideas about the oedipal situation, and its consequences for the structuring of the internal world, are proposed as a useful tool for formulating clinical objectives, selecting patients, and determining an appropriate group technique.

The Art and Science of Assessment in Psychotherapy. Edited by Chris Mace. Routledge. £40.00 (hb), £14.99 (pb). Pp. 222.

Nollaig Drake

Nollaig Drake

Nollaig Drake  

This book presents a comprehensive view of the theory and practice of assessment across a broad range of psychotherapies. There are contributions from sixteen distinguished clinicians, trainers and researchers, who draw upon a wide literature across the field of psychotherapy. From this point of view, the book provides a very useful resource for further exploration.

Individual chapters describe the assessment process for eight different types of therapy, including psychoanalytic psychotherapy, psychodrama and short-term cognitive behaviour therapy. There are also substantial chapters on couple therapy and focal therapy. Thus the book encompasses a wide range of therapeutic orientations, settings and interests, with the intention of stimulating comparative study that may enhance critical reflection.

Most clinicians would ordinarily have contact with only a fraction of the material in this book and the breadth of experience it represents. In this respect, the book is a timely contribution which reflects the recent developments in the requirements for the professional training of Specialist Registrars in Psychotherapy. It is expected that the consultant who meets a patient for assessment for psychotherapy will be equipped to make an informed decision about the most suitable psychotherapeutic intervention, including no intervention, for any particular individual. In order to do this, we need to be aware of the comparative contributions that each treatment modality can make, its indications and contraindications, its potential risks and benefits. This expectation also reflects the increasing demand for specificity in relation to treatment choices, in a Health Service where accountability and evidence of efficacy are high priorities. While this in itself seems quite a reasonable demand, it tends to obscure the fact that psychotherapy in the Health Service is a very under-funded resource. Thus, even if we can provide a more comprehensive training in assessment for the psychotherapies, we will continue to be confronted with the problem of locating the desired treatment resources, compounded by the additional difficulties with regard to the boundaries between different NHS contracting authorities. I mention these rather obvious points, lest we are led to believe that if only we could be better trained, more integrated, scientific and specific in our assessment procedures, then our patients would have a much better chance of getting the appropriate treatment. I am not convinced!

[[RunningHead]]

The Analyst's Preconscious. By Victoria Hamilton. The Analytic Press. $47.50/-£38.00. Pp. ix + 352.

Graham Ingham

Graham Ingham

Graham Ingham  

‘Meaning is use’ goes the philosophical slogan. It is a stance at the heart of psychoanalysis and firmly at the centre of this important book and the research study from

83

which it emerged. Victoria Hamilton's concern is ‘what analysts are actually doing’, and, connecting the thinking of all the analysts from whom we hear in the book is the belief that ‘what matters is intimately tied up with what works in the consulting room’. Securely located within the analytic tradition as it is, The Analyst's Preconscious is also an original and unusual work. Although deeply focused on the detail of analytic practice, it contains no clinical material—the ‘material’ comprises extracts from interviews with 65 British and American analysts (31 in London, 34 spread between New York, Los Angeles and San Francisco), all conducted by the author between 1988 and 1990.

The analysts interviewed are of differing orientations and seniority. They are presented anonymously (though the personalities of several emerge vividly in the course of the book, and it is difficult to avoid a guessing game), and they tell us how they work in response to Victoria Hamilton's semi-directed interviewing. The questions around which the interviews were structured are appendixed: they are sensitive, penetrating and finely-tuned instruments in themselves, and indeed a productive hour or two can be spent reflecting on one's own responses to them. They cover twenty-seven dimensions of technique: attitudes to neutrality, countertransference, etc., and were formulated over several years whilst Hamilton was at the Psychoanalysis Unit of the University of London. Her study is attempting to explore and map-out ‘what analysts draw on in order to enter into and elucidate the minds of others’. She views this territory as the preconscious, that region ‘neither unconscious nor fully conscious that makes each interpret and practise in his or her individual way.’ Its features are

the philosophical beliefs, the personal relationships, the important psychoanalytic theories and experience that animate therapeutic responsiveness.

Critical Review of the Review of Psychotherapy Services in England

David Taylor

David Taylor

120 Belsize Lane, London, NW3 5BA.

David Taylor  

This review of the Review has become something of an historical paper. It addresses the context and climate in which the Review's recommendations were originally formulated. The government has changed since the review was published in September 1996. With it, there have been some alterations in the political context which have rendered the points made a little, but only a little, less immediately with us. However, the potential of the human psyche to generate and to respond to the type of superego mental-functioning I try to describe remains, even if there are a few signs of lessening severity. For this reason, the points made retain some long-term relevance. Last but not least, the momentum established by the Psychotherapy Review offered a real opportunity for progress and development.

[[RunningHead]]

The William Inman Trust

Jennifer Johns

Jennifer Johns

Jennifer Johns  

I am here as Chairman of the William Inman Advisory Committee of the Council of the British Psycho-Analytical Society, which is supporting this conference, and also to give something of the background to this conference, and how it has come about that the British Psycho-Analytical Society should be collaborating with this distinguished hospital to discuss the topic which we have called The Mind's Eye. I will say something about a remarkable man, William Inman; but first I will describe psychoanalysis itself, and, since it can only be brief, my description will be incomplete. But perhaps it will do, at least to begin with.

Psychoanalysis is a term generally used to describe three things: a method of investigation; a body of theory; and a treatment—all interrelated. Historically, the method of investigation began when the neurological physician and anatomist Sigmund Freud, in 19th-century Vienna, discovered that his patients could, when lying down facing away from their physician, in a calm uninterrupted situation where they felt safe, gain access to and verbalise thoughts, feelings and memories that were not available to them in their ordinary lives, that were repressed or hidden on account of being unacceptable; and, frequently though not always, due to anxieties about sexual matters or violent feelings. Freud's genius was that he listened to his patients in this situation, and found a new level of understanding. He found that the apparently irrational or insignificant ideas or emotions that emerged from his relaxed patients could be seen to make links and patterns, that the responses to daily life could be seen to resonate with significant events in the patient's developmental history; and most importantly with the developing relationship to the doctor himself; and that these patterns held clues, and made links with the patient's psychopathology and symptomatology, both emotional and physical.

The Footballer, the Postman Pat Girl, and the Film-Maker

Mandy O'Keeffe

Mandy O'Keeffe

Mandy O'Keeffe   Medical Social Worker, Moorfields Eye Hospital

I would like to give just three brief case-studies to demonstrate some aspects of the multidisciplinary seminars. You will see that I am left wondering whether the medical teams and the social and psychological services could not, to advantage, be working more closely together.

Mrs Felton, a well-dressed, 50-something-year-old woman, arrived in my office. ‘I've taken him to the ward …,’ she said. There was a distinct feeling of ‘I've had enough of him; he's yours now’. She then embarked on a tirade against her husband. She hoped the operation would be successful, as his constant blinking and facial twitching drove her mad. Her husband had never visited her when she was in hospital, and he had even insisted on having sex with her now, when she was still recovering from her operation.

Her husband, she told me, was virtually blind, due to blepharospasm (a compulsive, violent blinking), and was to be admitted for a major operation. She gave a picture of a devoted wife to a selfish, very dependent and incompetent husband—and she was furious about it.

I had to see Mr Felton to discuss a practical matter.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Challenge of Attachment for Care-Giving. By Dorothy Heard & Brian Lake. Routledge. £45.00 (hb), £14.99 (pb). Pp. viii + 229

Penelope Turton

Penelope Turton

Penelope Turton  

This book sets out to integrate an inter-generational theory of caregiving and attachment with a personal and object relations theory of the self. The authors assert that attachment theory has become an indispensable component of a clinical bio-psycho-social theory of the human self. They further propose that the fundamental tenets of attachment theory, when integrated into a theory of the self, can be employed by therapists to understand their clients’ ‘predicaments’ and the defensive structures which pose obstacles to mature self-care and management. Finally, they suggest that the therapeutic task may be defined in attachment terms as providing a new model of caregiving, through which the client may be freed to exchange defensive ways of relating to the self, others and the world for mature and mutually satisfying ones.

Bowlby's model of instinctive behaviour, of which caregiving is a component, provides the theoretical grounding for the book. The authors argue that it provides the most solid foundation available for understanding how the relationship a child has with his parents can affect his wellbeing and the unfolding of his developing sense of self.

The book seeks to extend Bowlby's ideas by focusing on the concept of supportive companionable caregiving. In conceptualising this model of caregiving, and contrasting it with a more dysfunctional pattern, termed dominating submissive caregiving, the authors survey a broad sweep of literature. In particular, they comment on the contribution of Stern's work on attunement; Trevarthen's theory of intersubjectivity; MacLean's evolutionary perspective on non-verbal signals; the literature on the psychobiology of attachment and separation; Sperry's work on the neural infrastructure of brain processes mediating conscious awareness; and the burgeoning empirical work on attachment led by Main and others.

184

responseSet.title: Sigmund Freud. By Stephen Wilson. Stroud: Sutton Publishing (Pocket Biographies series).
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Sigmund Freud. By Stephen Wilson. Stroud: Sutton Publishing (Pocket Biographies series).

Jane Milton

Jane Milton

Jane Milton  

Stephen Wilson's last book was The Cradle of Violence (1995, Jessica Kingsley), a fascinating and diverse collection of his essays whose overall link was that of the unconscious roots of human behaviour. This is a book of quite a different kind, a slim volume that is one of an inexpensive paperback biographical series. Previous subjects have included Beethoven, Marilyn Munroe and Jane Austen; the Curies, Margot Fonteyn and Enid Blyton are to come. The challenge of writing a ‘pocket biography’ and to succeed in being concise without being superficial, is considerable, especially where the subject is one who has been exhaustively studied and written about by so many scholars. One has in addition a considerable responsibility, as the average reader of such a work is likely to be a casually interested lay person, nowadays probably one whose prior knowledge of Freud consists mostly of the frequent and wildly inaccurate character assassinations of the Sunday papers!

Wilson tells us that his main sources are the biographies of Jones and Gay. He succeeds admirably, I think, with broad and vivid brush-strokes, in illustrating his complex subject. Freud comes alive from the first pages, in his dignified retreat from the Nazis in 1938. Both his courage and ironic humour (‘I can most highly recommend the Gestapo to everyone’), his stubbornness, and his passionate involvement with friends, colleagues and family, come across well in Chapter One, ‘The Reluctant Refugee’.

Having started in this lively way near the end of Freud's life, Wilson takes us in more traditionally chronological order, starting with Chapter Two ‘Boyhood’, and moving swiftly and concisely through the life of both the man and the pioneer discoverer. Inevitably this reader would have liked more detail of both, but the important point is that Wilson's writing does whet the appetite for more, and it is to be hoped that he will engage readers to pursue further.

186

responseSet.title: Editorial
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Editorial

Jane Milton

Jane Milton

Jane Milton  

Viewing NHS Psychotherapy Services at the 50-year mark, we know how often our minimally-resourced clinics are faced with patients whose difficulties challenge the limits of our treatments. We often can provide only a fraction of what we may judge the patient really needs. This regularly faces us with clinical and ethical dilemmas. When is it right, even safe, to offer once-weekly, time-limited treatment to a deeply disturbed person who ideally needs the containment of a full analysis? Is it best to offer nothing or too little? The difficulty and discomfort for both patient and therapist of sustaining analytic work when little time can be given may make packaged, more ‘educational’ or cognitive treatments seem misleadingly attractive, though some would argue that the patient is thereby sold even shorter.

Three papers in this issue of Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy cause us to consider such important themes by their presentations of work with borderline and psychotic patients. Richard Lucas presents an unusual and impressive account of the NHS treatment of a manic-depressive patient in full analysis. The availability of a sufficiently containing structure and appropriate expert resources for this is almost unheard of. It is worth noting, however, that this treatment, though labour-intensive, is relatively inexpensive compared with in-patient treatment, and could quickly pay its way in selected cases if overall admission time was thereby shortened even fractionally. Judith Felman's thoughtful account of once-weekly work with a severely depressed self-destructive patient raises interesting questions about the limits of treatability. Felman uses her case to think about the clinical usefulness of the death-instinct concept. Penelope Garvey's paper is also about work with borderline patients, and her vivid case-material is used to think about Rosenfeld's work on narcissistic states.

Many patients in the NHS are treated in analytic groups, and contributions to Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy on group work are disproportionately few. Michael Halton's paper is thus most welcome, and timely in view of the recent launch of the Group Section of the APP. Halton looks critically and afresh at both Bion's and Foulkes's theories of group technique. He argues for an approach which gives greater emphasis to the transference relationship with the therapist, particularly around ubiquitously-present oedipal issues.

responseSet.title: Letters to the Editor
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Letters to the Editor

From Mr James McDonald

I was interested to read the paper, Curious Bedfellows: Psychoanalytic understanding and old age psychiatry, by Mark Ardern, Jane Garner & Ruth Porter (Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy 12, 47-56). I would like to comment on two matters.

Loss can, indeed, be a central feature in the lives of old people: losses, sometimes seemingly unmourned, can from a very early age be cumulative in nature. But those listed in the paper are, in fact, not ‘age-specific’: loss of family, friends and pets, for example, can occur at any age. One profound loss that is ‘age-specific’, but is not mentioned is that of hope. Although one may perhaps cling to some remnant of hope, it is likely to be of little comfort for old people if not supported by any realistic expectation of fulfilment. Thinking about and planning for the future which in a spirit of optimism can sustain younger people is largely denied them. Hope dwindles with the increasing sense of time running out. I do not know the ages of the writers but I wonder if perhaps older therapists are likely to be more readily able to empathise with old people in this particular loss which, though ‘age-specific’, is omitted from the list given.

It may be that dreams sometimes afford comfort as it is said they did for Mrs Q. However, my experience has been that this is not always the case, and I think it is important to acknowledge that they can also be an additional source of grief, poignantly reviving the sad sense of loss. One old lady recently told me that a loving dream of her long-dead husband, which brought her warmth and happiness within the dream, resulted in intensely sad feelings of loss the next day. The comfort given in the dream was not, for her, worth the heavy emotional cost she later had to pay and, given a choice, she would elect not to have such dreams.

As Rupert Brooke says in the poem Sometimes Even Now.

It does but double the heart-ache

When I wake, when I wake.

Yours faithfully

JAMES T MCDONALD

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Letters to the Editor

From Drs Mark Ardern, Jane Garner & Ruth Porter

We enjoyed reading James McDonald's letter written in response to our paper and would like to thank him for his thoughtful comments. He is of course correct in pointing out that losses are not age-specific but occur throughout life. We wish to emphasise that in old age losses multiply and accumulate:

When sorrows come, they come not single spies,

But in battalions ………—Hamlet IV, Sc.5, 78.

We disagree, however, that loss of hope is necessarily age-specific. It reflects a depressive state of mind. In a realistic way the healthy older person still plans for the future in a spirit of optimism but the time-span is shorter and the plans less ambitious. A grandparent watching his grandchildren play and run no longer wishes to do this himself but enjoys watching and remembering when he did the same. Unconscious understanding promotes conscious acceptance of what is, rather than what might have been: acceptance of what is, rather than false hope for the impossible.

We agree that therapists at different stages in their own lives may bring different qualities and fears to the relationship but empathy is thought to be a personal characteristic not related to age.

The dream described in our paper was a manifestation of the patient's unconscious. The change in the dream engendered through therapy helped her mourn and see her life more realistically. On reflection, an important aspect was not only the actual dream but the fact that the patient brought that dream to her therapist and what it meant in the transference.

We are grateful that James McDonald's letter has given us the opportunity to think further about these issues.

Yours faithfully

MARK ARDERN

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Letters to the Editor

From Dr Phil Mollon

Subtle disturbances of thought, easily overlooked, are common in contemporary discussions of trauma, memory and abuse. Evidence of these pervade Cleo Van Velsen's review of my book Multiple Selves, Multiple Voices and Showalter's Hystories (Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy 12, 163-171). The result, even though no doubt well-intentioned, falls short of expected standards of accurate representation and coherence of argument in a number of worrying ways.

One example is where Van Velsen quotes a section of my book describing the experience of working with very severely disturbed patients presenting narratives of perverse abuse:

My experience is of feeling burdened—by communications of bewilderingly bizarre evil, the truth of which I have no way of knowing. [Mollon]

Immediately after quoting this, she asserts:

This statement links to what Showalter describes, namely moving from a realm of objectivity and rationality … to one of belief … [Van Velsen]

She then goes on to say:

I do not see it as the psychoanalytical therapist's job to be the patient's advocate or the true believer … [Van Velsen]

Thus, because I state that I do not know what to make of narratives of ritual abuse, unlike Van Velsen who believes they are not true, she places me in a category she terms ‘true believer’ and describes me as a ‘patient's advocate’. In fact, as the book indicates, I stand for a position of disciplined enquiry, which aims to avoid falling into states of mind characterised by ‘belief’.

Later, Van Velsen remarks:

I think that MPD is a worrying diagnosis, in the same way that alien abduction is, or satanic ritual abuse is.

Letters to the Editor

From Dr Cleo Van Velsen

My one particular comment is a wish to correct any misunderstanding in the paragraph referring to the British Psycho-Analytical Society and a seminar group. I used the phrase ‘in the tradition of …’ as a way of describing British psychoanalytic thinking, which Dr Mollon discusses in his book. I felt free to refer to the Society as it is the only one affiliated to the International Psychoanalytical Association and therefore represents official psychoanalysis in the UK.

When describing ‘a’ seminar group I meant this in a general sense and for it to link to the passage quoted on the same page where Dr Mollon refers to being in a seminar. I had no one particular seminar group in mind and regret any misunderstanding.

Otherwise I can only express my regret that a serious, academic review of an important and contentious subject has been reacted, and responded, to so personally.

Yours sincerely

CLEO VAN VELSEN

Consultant Psychiatrist in Psychotherapy

The Maudsley, London.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychotic States in Children. Edited by M Rustin, M Rhode, A Dubinsky & H Dubinsky. Tavistock Clinic Series. Duckworth. £12.95. Pp. viii + 293.

Alan Shuttleworth

Alan Shuttleworth

Alan Shuttleworth  

This is a collection of papers by staff and graduates of the Tavistock Child Psychotherapy Training, plus a paper by Genevieve Haag. Ten of the papers describe extended psychoanalytic work with very disturbed children, in each case giving a picture of the whole course of treatment of a child with detailed material describing major themes and moments. Papers by Maria Rhode and Haag each group together some of their work with different children. There is a very full account by Sue Reid of one session with a child. There are four mainly theoretical discussion/overview chapters (by Alex Dubinsky, Alex and Helene Dubinsky, Maria Rhode and Margaret Rustin). There is a theoretical glossary by Rhode, and an Introduction and a Postscript to the whole collection by Rustin. The volume both presents a good deal of clinical work and sets out to draw general and theoretical themes from it.

The accumulation of accounts of individual children is clinically rich and often moving. Some of those papers which offer accounts of the whole course of the work with a child—a style of paper much loved by child psychotherapists—are especially moving in the intimate sense they give of these prolonged, deep, shaping encounters. The best of these papers both bring considerable understanding to bear, and, at the same time, communicate a sense of the full weight and shock of these often immensely difficult contacts, where strong but distressing attachments form. In many of these papers, it is made clear that, while some of the impact of these encounters was well grasped by theory, some parts of what took place altogether eluded understanding. In her clinical paper, Rustin addresses directly and courageously the difficulty involved in facing the limits to her understanding and helpfulness at the end of the very long process of therapy with one child.

The overall form of the book is that of a dialogue between chapters that are mainly theory and chapters that are mainly clinical. The mainly theoretical part of this dialogue is Kleinian. Its principal endeavour is to try to clarify and consolidate the connections between the kind of clinical practice with children that is presented here; the Kleinian tradition of clinical work with psychotic adults; and the main lines of theory to which that adult work has given rise. Theoretically, the book turns on the view that the term psychotic can be applied in psychoanalytic work with children in more or less just the sense that it is applied in adult work. But the sense of psychotic in adult psychoanalytic work is closely linked to its parallel use in adult psychiatry to refer, principally, to schizophrenia and manic depression. Since these adult disorders are not found prior to adolescence, if the term psychotic is to be applied to childhood in a helpful way, it cannot be in a way that carries quite the same reference as when used in adult work.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Counselling, Psychotherapy and the Law. By Peter Jenkins. London: Sage Publications. Pp. 340. £14.99.

Anne Zachary

Anne Zachary

Anne Zachary  

This is a valuable handbook with reference sections and tables which are easy to use. It sets out to challenge the threat of ‘legislation of therapy’ feared by Bollas and Sundelson in The New Informants (1995), with an alternative approach that an understanding of the law, and a closer relationship with it, represents something of an opportunity for therapists to further development by entering a debate. The book's author attended the first conference of the British Confederation of Psychotherapists (BCP) in 1995, which had a similar aim. Tim Bond, a previous chairman of the British Association for Counselling, in his foreword, states that ignorance of the law is no defence, but goes on to observe that ethical standards tend to meet legal requirements, even without a prior extensive knowledge of the law.

A threat to the confidentiality of our work as psychotherapists commonly sets off a highly charged reaction in the therapist in the interests of its preservation. However, this reaction should not be at the expense of thinking and reason, and in any individual situation it must be kept clear what is being discussed. Under the blanket term ‘confidentiality’, this often becomes blurred. This book manages to set out very clearly the different areas under consideration. The main ones are: where the therapist is custodian of confidential information and whether or not in certain circumstances which are precisely defined there are grounds for disclosure (Chapter 4: Confidentiality, Privilege and the Public Interest); and the opposite situation, where external agencies are demanding disclosure (Chapter 5: Access to Records and Disclosure of Information). The latter situation can include the patient's wish to see his own records. These two chapters thus define the limits of confidentiality. It is surprising how entangled these separate areas can become, in a discussion where one party may be worrying about the former and the other about the latter.

These two central chapters give a clear and objective account of each party's rights in various situations which might arise. Regarding the therapist's dilemma when wanting to preserve confidentiality, the concept of professional or clinical judgment is paramount. Box 4.8 on page 147 gives the bottom line: that there is only one situation in which a therapist must pass on information, and that is to prevent terrorism. In all other situations, the therapist may be required to pass on information. Here the author is referring to child-abuse, serious crime and drug-trafficking. (This is not strictly accurate where doctors are concerned in relation to drugs and certain communicable diseases where there are also statutory regulations. Arguably these would rarely be encountered by the doctor working in role as a psychotherapist). Prevention of such disclosures ever being necessary is achieved by good professional practice. Box 4.7 on page 145, ‘Guidelines for Professional Practice regarding Confidentiality’, sets out a suggested plan. This entails developing a confidentiality policy, discussing its implications with colleagues and staff, and providing within it legitimate professional limitations (as described above).

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Clinical Thinking of Wilfred Bion. By Joan & Neville Symington. Routledge £40.00 (hb), 14.99 (pb). Pp. xiv + 198.

Philip Stokoe

Philip Stokoe

Philip Stokoe  

I have two reactions to this book. The more important is that it is an impressively clear and thoughtful description of Bion's clinical thinking; the other is a disturbing irritation at what appears to be a sub-plot, an attempt to claim ownership of those very ideas. I shall deal with these two reactions separately.

I am not a Bion scholar, although I feel that I have been very influenced by his work and ideas; consequently I am not in a position to argue with the Symingtons’ presentation when they are explaining his concepts and how these concepts connect and link together. They approach in an extraordinary way the problem of presenting ideas which many find obscure and complex, and yet they pull it off. They begin with the Grid. There must be very few of us who feel that we can explain what the Grid is all about, because it seems to be very abstract and quasi-mathematical. It is the part of Bion that most of us try not to notice, because it seems even more obscure than his writing. And yet, by beginning with this, Joan & Neville Symington manage to bring together all of Bion's later thinking in a cohesive and interesting way. We are helped by clear and simple clinical material to understand that this Grid represents a way of defining what is going on in the psychoanalytical work, the way that statements by either analyst or patient can be understood as either blocking or developing that work. The vertical axis represents the increasing development of thinking, while the horizontal axis is about the attitude or intention of the participant.

With continuous reference to the Grid, which becomes more and more understandable, we are taken on a journey, the journey of psychic development as conceived by Bion.

180

responseSet.title: Who Cares? True Stories of the NHS Reforms. By Peter Bruggen. London: Jon Carpenter Publishing. Pp. 314.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Who Cares? True Stories of the NHS Reforms. By Peter Bruggen. London: Jon Carpenter Publishing. Pp. 314.

Philip Stokoe

Philip Stokoe

Philip Stokoe  

Peter Bruggen is a well-respected psychiatrist and psychoanalyst who had been the consultant psychiatrist at Hill End Adolescent Unit for twenty-five years and at the Tavistock Clinic for twenty, until his retirement in 1994. By that time, he had also been a medical director of an NHS Trust. It is with this commitment to the NHS in mind that we should read this book. I read it with particular interest because a large part of my work, both in private practice and at the Tavistock Clinic is focused on helping those working in the NHS to work more effectively and to understand the pressures on them which aim to stop their thinking and interfere with best practice. In the last five years or so, I have had to include, in my support work with staff, the impact upon them of their managers and the Trusts for which they work. This is a significant change, because, with very few exceptions, if issues were brought into discussion which pertained to the institution or the management, I used to be pretty sure that this was a projection in line with Bion's basic-assumption fight/flight, and that the real difficulties originated in the work with the patients or staff dynamics within the staff team. Dr Bruggen's book, a collection of the experiences of various staff from the NHS, gives vivid and moving evidence that the institutions of the NHS have become the source of an attack of great destructive power on the good practice of the work-force.

The book begins with a brief but clear and helpful summary of the history of the changes in the NHS. I thought it was a fair and balanced account of the changes and reforms as well as an indication of the attitudes of those behind the changes. This is followed by twenty-four chapters, each describing a story (sometime more than one, but related) which comes from a careful process of interviewing and checking. At the end of the book there is a conclusion, but this is not an analysis or an attempt to explain; instead, Bruggen seems to want to let us draw our own conclusions. I think that this is absolutely right: it means that he can present the events simply and as they stand. His style is deceptively simple but the impact is powerful.

responseSet.title: Problems in the Management of Borderline Patients in In-Patient Settings
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Problems in the Management of Borderline Patients in In-Patient Settings

Marcus Evans

Marcus Evans

Grove Lane, London SE5 8SN.

Marcus Evans  

In this paper some difficulties in the management of borderline patients being treated on two specialist inpatient units will be explored in the light of changes in the health-care system. On these units, nursing staff developed beliefs about care, based on previous experience of difficulties in the clinical area. In many ways, these assumptions about the patient group were based on common-sense. However, when prescribed indiscriminantly, they became ‘over-valued’ ideas, which acted as a defence against the patients’ communications. Nurses stopped thinking about the meaning of the patients’ behaviour, and based their actions and behaviour on pre-selected understanding connected with their own defensive needs. The process of introjection and projection necessary for containment was short-circuited, and nurses acted as if they knew what the patients’ behaviour meant, rather than wait for meaning to emerge. Despite the patients’ denial of their pathology, when they feel their carers have lost touch with the level of their psychological difficulties, they act out in ways which force the nursing-team into action. When the nursing-team reacts without thinking about the meaning of the patients’ behaviour, a mindless and often destructive cycle of action and reaction takes place between staff and patients. This cycle goes against the philosophy of both units, which is to help patients to verbalise rather than act-out their difficulties.
