responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Footballer, the Postman Pat Girl, and the Film-Maker

Liz Middleton

Liz Middleton

Liz Middleton   Orthoptist, Middlesex Hospital

As things currently stand, we do our patients a disservice by working at a polarised end of the Cartesian split. We choose not to see what is in front of us. It is us, the eye professionals, who turn a blind eye.

Billy the Footballer

I shall start with Billy, a robust outgoing 9-year-old, fond of football, who was referred to the Eye Department, complaining of blurred and deteriorating vision.

His period of attendance at the Eye Clinic ran concurrently with my participation at the Tavistock Eye seminars.

He was seen firstly by an orthoptic colleague in March. Billy was found to have 6/18 vision in both eyes, somewhat less than the desired standard of 6/6. The consultant ophthalmologist had found: no refractive error, white and quiet eyes, and healthy discs. When I saw Billy, I found his vision to be initially 6/18 6/12. His convergence was slightly reduced. I had a feeling his vision was higher than 6/18 6/12. With further testing, with a variety of different very weak lenses used to correct long-sightedness, short-sightedness, or astigmatism, his vision came up indiscriminately to 6/5 6/5, suggesting that optically at least his vision was sound.

Standard Orthoptic case-history taking involves making enquiries about ocular history, signs and symptoms, a possible family history of squint, refractive error and other eye disorders; establishing if there are any general-health problems, and finding out details about the birth history. In Billy's case, however, I felt it prudent to make a few sensitive enquiries about his school and family lives. He came with both his parents, although they were divorced. Both parents had remarried, and Billy now had a little step-brother. On their way out of the door, his mother volunteered the information that Billy's grandmother had a brain-tumour.

I brought Billy's case to the Tavistock Eye group. I pointed out that at the hospital his divorced parents were together. I wondered if there was anxiety about the divorce and the grandmother's brain-tumour.

[[RunningHead]]

The Death Instinct: A Psychological Killer

Judith Felman

Judith Felman

5 Oakwood Park Road, London, N14 6QB.

Judith Felman  

This paper considers the impact that a once-a-week therapy had on a patient with a borderline-narcissistic type of personality organisation. The patient described, a 50-year-old woman, came to treatment following a depressive breakdown. The three-year period of psychotherapy seemed only to have made her de-compensate even further; her defensive organisation continued to weaken. Yet, with the announcement of the end of treatment, a recovery started to occur. The theory of the life and death instincts, from its conceptualisation and development by Freud, through its later evolution, is used as a conceptual framework to understand the powerful struggle within this individual between those forces pulling towards growth and development, and those pulling towards destructiveness and deterioration. The case is discussed not only with this theory in mind, but in the context of personality structure and of what happens when a patient's defensive organisation is diminished through therapy. The ‘pros and cons’ of psychotherapy are considered, with special attention being paid to its implications for time-limited therapy within the NHS.

[[RunningHead]]

Recovered Memories of Trauma: Transferring the Present to the Past. By C Brooks Brenneis. Madison, Connecticut: Int. Univ. Press. Pp. 204.

Christopher Cordess

Christopher Cordess

Christopher Cordess  

This book, by a psychoanalyst well known for previous work on trauma and dreams, provides an excellent and balanced overview of the contentious debate concerning the validity—or otherwise—of memories recovered in adulthood of childhood trauma and sexual abuse. It is also a challenge to psychoanalysts, psychotherapists and related professions to look long and hard at certain time-honoured dogmas which urgently need revision. The issues are highly complex in conceptual and clinical terms. In addition, they demand a wider knowledge of current cognitive psychology, and specifically of memory research, than psychotherapists have traditionally received in their training. As Brenneis says in his Preface,

Very often the dispute about recovered memories is framed as occurring between clinically naive researchers and research naive clinicians

and he sets himself the task of reviewing and integrating the best of both research and clinical knowledge into a considered judgment of the current state of the art.

Psychoanalysts, as well as other therapists basing their work upon psychoanalytic principles, until recent times, had used outdated models of memory, based largely on assumptions of underlying veridical and permanent records of the past—palimpsests of actuality, as it were—which led some to dangerous credulity in matters of ‘recovered memories’. In present times, by contrast, the practice of most psychoanalysts is one of scepticism in regard to the psychic products of the interweaving of unconscious phantasy and historical ‘fact’ as represented, for example, in the greater emphasis on narrative as opposed to historical truth.

However, the subtle influences, and not so subtle pressures, of the ‘trauma movement’—which has become a dominant force in psychiatry as well as in the psychodynamic therapies—threatens, I believe, dangerously to distort a difficult technical dilemma by over-simplification, with potentially calamitous results. Also, there has been a failure amongst psychodynamic therapists to distinguish between the many kinds of memory, eg ‘explicit’, ‘autobiographical’ or ‘declarative’, memory (conscious) on the one hand, and ‘implicit’—including ‘procedural’—memory (more related to ‘unconscious phantasy’) on the other. Confusion abounds when therapists, as well possibly as their patients, assume capacities of brain systems and neuro-cognitive function which are empirically unlikely—if not impossible. For example, implicit (procedural memories)—wherein much childhood memory is held—cannot achieve verbal representation without ‘reconstruction’. The resultant recovered memory is more likely to be (historically speaking) ‘false’, than ‘true’. Although, from a certain strictly psychoanalytic perspective this differentiation is immaterial, it is from this area that much of the ‘recovered memory’ furore and its consequences have sprung. For an excellent overview offering a bridge between up-to-date neuro-physiological and neuropsychological knowledge, and psychoanalytic practice, see Pally (1997).

[[RunningHead]]

The Eye and I: Psychological Aspects of Common Eye Disorders

Alexis Brook

Alexis Brook

3 Eton College Road, London NW3 2BS.

Alexis Brook   Consulting Psychotherapist, Tavistock Clinic

The title of this conference, The Mind's Eye, draws attention to the connection between the eye and the mind. This connection is vividly reflected in everyday language. To make eye-contact means making a relationship, and seeing eye-to-eye means experiencing mutual understanding. We can look with love and we can look with hate. The evil eye can injure by a look, and looks can kill. ‘I see’ means ‘I understand’. To have one's eyes open is to be emotionally and intellectually aware of what is going on. But if there is something we do not want to bear in mind we lose sight of it. Or if there is something we do not want to acknowledge, because it may be too painful, we turn a blind eye. An unpleasant truth can hit you in the eye. Giving insight means giving internal sight with the eyes of the mind.

The eye is not just an organ of vision, a human camera, but is one of the most basic organs through which an individual relates to the world. After the mouth, the eye is the most important organ through which a baby and its mother make contact with each other. It is surprising, therefore, that psychological aspects of eye disorders are such a neglected field.

The late Mrs Inman's bequest to the British Psycho-Analytical Society to further her husband's pioneer work is, therefore, as Jennifer Johns has made clear, very welcome. I am particularly grateful that I was asked to initiate the project and have now seen over a hundred patients suffering from common eye disorders. I shall first give a brief overall description of my work. I shall then give clinical material to illustrate some of the stresses that seem, among the numerous medical factors, to contribute to the development, and sometimes to the recurrence, or the perpetuation, of many of these disorders. Finally, I shall discuss my findings.

Mrs Inman had requested that some work should be carried out in her husband's old hospital, Queen Alexandra, Portsmouth. Peter Fenton, the senior ophthalmic surgeon at the time, had always had an interest in the emotional aspects of eye disorders and was keen for work to be carried out there. For two years I visited his Department for two days a month. This was then complemented by work in a primary-care setting: for the past three years I have been visiting the Well Street practice in Hackney, where I have been working in collaboration with Dr Zalidis who will be speaking later. I have seen about fifty patients in each setting, ranging in age from 9 to 87, two-thirds of them women.

Curious Bedfellows: Psychoanalytic Understanding and Old Age Psychiatry

Mark Ardern , Jane Garner and Ruth Porter

Ruth Porter

London W10 6DZ.

Enfield EN2 8JL.

Psychotherapist, London.

Ruth Porter  

At first sight, a psychoanalytic perspective on clinical work may seem to some to have nothing to offer the old age psychiatrist and his team. However, psychiatrists’ daily work involves unconscious as well as conscious phenomena. In this paper the present value and future possibilities of a psychoanalytic understanding are emphasised, both in terms of therapy for individual patients and also to inform other work within the department. Clinical material is introduced to illustrate these ideas. It is suggested that psychoanalytic psychotherapy is used not to replace other treatments, but as an adjunct to biological, social and other psychological approaches, to the benefit of individual patients, staff, and the service itself.

Lost for Words: The Psychoanalysis of Anorexia and Bulimia. By Em Farrell. London: Process Press. £9.95. Pp. xv + 104.

Brett Karr

Brett Karr

Brett Karr  

In December of 1892 and January of 1893, Freud published a brief communication, in two parts, translated as A Case of Successful Treatment by Hypnotism: With Some Remarks on the Origin of Hysterical Symptoms Through ‘Counter-Will’ (Freud 1892-93), arguable the first psychoanalytical contribution to the study of eating disorders, describing the case of a primiparous mother who developed loss of appetite after her newborn baby experienced feeding difficulties. Freud treated this young woman with hypnotic suggestion, and after the series of interventions, both the mother and her child fed successfully.

Since the appearance of Freud's article, the psychoanalytical understanding and treatment of the eating disorders has become a significant field of enquiry in its own right, with a large body of literature, and justly so, because anorexic and bulimic patients experience a great deal of psychic turmoil, and many can benefit vastly from professional help. Not only do these individuals suffer psychologically, but many of them live on the brink of death as a result of the physical sequelae of eating disorders.

[[RunningHead]]

The Contemporary Kleinians of London. Edited by Roy Schafer. Madison: International Universities Press. $65.00, £62.00. Pp. ix + 442.

Julian Stern

Julian Stern

Julian Stern  

Roy Schafer, an eminent training analyst in New York, has long been interested in Kleinian psychoanalysis. As he explains in the epilogue to this book, in the 1950s he was

indoctrinated to consider Kleinian analysis a wildly speculative system basing itself on inadequate evidence and giving rise to unverifiable interpretations; also as a mythology or demonology that was taking Freud's already discredited death instinct speculations too seriously and literally

(p. 427).

Over the next forty years, he has endeavoured to look at Kleinian meta-theory and writings and help others

re-open closed eyes and ears, not only to a valuable body of analytic contributions but also to the rich, primitive world of object relational phantasy

(p. 429).

This book is his way of introducing these authors, who he has struggled so hard to de-demonise and value for himself, to a wider, predominantly American, audience.

The book itself is a collection of eighteen papers by twelve authors. Some (Segal, Steiner, Feldman, and Anderson) have contributed two, and Betty Joseph, three, papers. Of the eighteen papers, all have previously been published, except the two from Robin Anderson; and, of those previously published, eight are from the 1980s and another eight from the 1990s. The various chapters are brought together, initially by Schafer's interesting introduction, ‘The Contemporary Kleinians of London’ (previously published in 1994 in Psychoanalytic Quarterly) and thereafter by his useful, smaller, introductory comments to each article. In this way, Schafer is following the tradition of previous collections of Kleinian works, including Spillius's two-volume set, Melanie Klein Today (1988), Joseph's selected papers, Psychic Equilibrium and Psychic Change (1989), and Steiner's Psychic Retreats (1993), to mention a few. He is generous in his indebtedness to many of the above authors for their help.

The editor restricts himself to authors living and working in London, thus excluding figures such as Bion and Rosenfeld (deceased), and Henri Rey (no longer in London). What the book makes no claim to being is a comprehensive review of the development of Kleinian thought. For this the interested reader will still need to return to Spillius's two volumes (1988), or her more recent personal view (1994).

[[RunningHead]]

Bringing Deadness to Life: An Appreciation of Rosenfeld's Contribution to Thinking about Narcissism11

Penelope Garvey

Penelope Garvey

Leyhill, Payhembury, Honiton, Devon, EX14 0JG.

Penelope Garvey  

In this paper I have described some of Rosenfeld's ideas about narcissism, and in particular the distinction he makes between libidinal and destructive narcissism. Using a clinical example to illustrate the struggle between dependency and the power of excitement and destruction, I have attempted to show some narcissistic defences in action. I have argued that while Rosenfeld's differentiation may not hold, in that both constructive and destructive elements are always present, he has identified vividly important elements. The degree of the destructiveness, the extent to which it is split-off, hidden and unavailable, the extent to which it is maintained by excitement and eroticisation, and the rigidity of the organisation that has been set up (that is, where or not much is allowed in from outside) are all crucial. What needs to be distinguished are states of mind, and which state of mind is in the ascendancy at any one time. I have reviewed briefly some of the theories advanced to explain why these defences come into existence, and I have mentioned some of the consequent implications for technique.

Cultural Pluralism and Psychoanalysis: the Asian and North American Experience. By Alan Roland. New York and London: Routledge. Pp. 218.

M Davids

M Davids

M Davids  

This is a fascinating book. Alan Roland is a psychoanalyst with extensive experience of treating Indian and Japanese patients in New York. He also lived for a number of years in India and Japan, where he undertook psychoanalytic research. He therefore has excellent credentials for exploring the extent to which psychoanalysis can adapt to a world in which cultural pluralism is becoming more and more a fact of life. For us, this is of more than academic interest, for as we are increasingly called upon to see patients from minority ethnic groups, we will inevitably have to consider which aspects of our theory and technique are relevant to them, and which might be said to constitute an unwitting cultural imposition which, of course, would run counter to the whole spirit of the psychoanalytic approach.

Roland's book addresses the question of whether psychoanalysis is properly equipped to understand and treat patients who come from cultural groupings other than those of European extraction. Broadly, his answer is no. Although it claims universal validity for its models of the mind, Roland argues that, as a product of Western thought, psychoanalysis is in fact built on hidden values that revolve around what he calls individualism. This is a set of values (whose trajectory he traces back to the Reformation) that permeates Western society, underpinning a mode of living in which the individual is regarded as the self-contained unit of society.

If individuals are set on their own in society in a way never before done, then psychoanalysis is oriented toward enabling them to be on their own by resolving all kinds of inner conflicts and deficits. What other psychological theory is so thorough-going in its exploration of the individual psyche, or is so oriented towards individuals taking full responsibility for their lives through resolving the dictates of the unconscious?
